question: What was the Central Government Debt as a percentage of GDP in FY 2022-23?

expected.answer_summary: The Central Government Debt as a percentage of GDP was 57.93% at the end of FY 2022-23.
key_facts: Central Government debt as percentage of GDP was 57.93% in FY 2022-23
This represented a decline from 58.76% in FY 2021-22

key_amounts.context: Central Government Debt as percentage of GDP in FY 2022-23

question: By how much did the total Central Government debt increase in FY 2022-23?

expected.answer_summary: The Central Government debt increased by ₹17.48 lakh crore or 12.61% over FY 2021-22, primarily due to an accumulation of internal debt of ₹16.12 lakh crore.
key_facts: Central Government debt increased by ₹17.48 lakh crore in FY 2022-23
This represented a 12.61% increase over the previous year
Increase was largely due to internal debt increase of ₹16.12 lakh crore

key_amounts.context: increase in Central Government debt in FY 2022-23
percentage increase in Central Government debt
increase in internal debt

question: What are the FRBM targets for Central Government Debt and General Government Debt?

expected.answer_summary: The FRBM Act targets are to limit the Central Government Debt to 40% of GDP and the General Government Debt to 60% of GDP by the end of FY 2024-25.
key_facts: Central Government Debt target is 40% of GDP
General Government Debt target is 60% of GDP
Both targets are to be achieved by end of FY 2024-25

key_amounts.context: Central Government Debt target as percentage of GDP
General Government Debt target as percentage of GDP

question: What was the amount of tax revenue raised but not realized at the end of FY 2022-23?

expected.answer_summary: At the end of FY 2022-23, ₹21.30 lakh crore was raised as taxes but yet to be realized, which was 69.74% of the gross tax collected during the year. This represented an increase of ₹5.47 lakh crore over the previous year.
key_facts: Tax revenue raised but not realized was ₹21.30 lakh crore
This was 69.74% of gross tax collected during the year
The unrealized amount increased by ₹5.47 lakh crore from FY 2021-22
Of the increase, ₹5.28 lakh crore was not under dispute

key_amounts.context: tax revenue raised but not realized
increase in unrealized tax revenue over previous year
percentage of gross tax collected

question: What was the Fiscal Deficit for FY 2022-23?

expected.answer_summary: The Fiscal Deficit for FY 2022-23 was ₹17.56 lakh crore, which was 6.51% of GDP. Of this, ₹10.71 lakh crore (61%) was towards funding deficits on revenue account.
key_facts: Fiscal Deficit was ₹17.56 lakh crore in FY 2022-23
Fiscal Deficit was 6.51% of GDP
Revenue Deficit component was ₹10.71 lakh crore (61% of Fiscal Deficit)

key_amounts.context: Fiscal Deficit for FY 2022-23
Fiscal Deficit as percentage of GDP

question: What was the total outstanding guarantee at the end of FY 2022-23 and was the additional guarantee within FRBM limits?

expected.answer_summary: The total outstanding guarantee at the end of FY 2022-23 was ₹3.14 lakh crore, which was 1.2% of GDP. Additional guarantees issued during FY 2022-23 were ₹0.61 lakh crore or 0.23% of GDP, which was well within the FRBM limit of 0.5% of GDP.
key_facts: Outstanding guarantee at end of FY 2022-23 was ₹3.14 lakh crore
Outstanding guarantee was 1.2% of GDP
Additional guarantees during FY 2022-23 were ₹0.61 lakh crore
Additional guarantee was 0.23% of GDP
FRBM limit for additional guarantee is 0.5% of GDP
Additional guarantee was within the FRBM limit

key_amounts.context: outstanding guarantee at end of FY 2022-23
additional guarantees during FY 2022-23
additional guarantee as percentage of GDP
FRBM limit for additional guarantee

question: How was the Fiscal Deficit financed in FY 2022-23?

expected.answer_summary: The Fiscal Deficit of ₹17.56 lakh crore in FY 2022-23 was financed mainly from: (1) Net internal debt - ₹16.12 lakh crore (91.80%), (2) External debt - ₹0.54 lakh crore (3.07%), (3) Public Account - ₹0.92 lakh crore (5.24%), and (4) Cash draw down - ₹0.02 lakh crore.
key_facts: Internal debt financed 91.80% of fiscal deficit
Internal debt contribution was ₹16.12 lakh crore
External debt share increased from 2.89% in FY 2021-22 to 3.07% in FY 2022-23
Public Account contributed ₹0.92 lakh crore (5.24%)

key_amounts.context: net internal debt used to finance fiscal deficit
share of internal debt in financing fiscal deficit
external debt used to finance fiscal deficit
share of external debt in financing fiscal deficit

question: What was the General Government Debt as percentage of GDP and how did it compare to previous years?

expected.answer_summary: The General Government Debt was 81.35% of GDP in FY 2022-23, down from 87.68% during the pandemic year FY 2020-21 but still higher than the pre-pandemic 70.39% in FY 2018-19. The total General Government Debt was ₹219.23 lakh crore at end of FY 2022-23. The target under FRBM is 60% by FY 2024-25.
key_facts: General Government Debt was 81.35% of GDP in FY 2022-23
It peaked at 87.68% during FY 2020-21 pandemic year
Pre-pandemic level was 70.39% in FY 2018-19
Total General Government Debt was ₹219.23 lakh crore
Target under FRBM is 60% by FY 2024-25

key_amounts.context: General Government Debt as percentage of GDP in FY 2022-23
General Government Debt as percentage of GDP in FY 2020-21
total General Government Debt in FY 2022-23

question: What was the capital expenditure in FY 2022-23 and how did it compare to previous years?

expected.answer_summary: Capital expenditure in FY 2022-23 was ₹6.25 lakh crore (13.73% of total expenditure), an increase of 16.17% over FY 2021-22 (₹5.38 lakh crore). It had declined to ₹3.43 lakh crore in FY 2020-21 from ₹4.0 lakh crore in FY 2018-19, but recovered significantly in subsequent years.
key_facts: Capital expenditure was ₹6.25 lakh crore in FY 2022-23
Capital expenditure was 13.73% of total expenditure
Increased by 16.17% over FY 2021-22
Capital expenditure in FY 2021-22 was ₹5.38 lakh crore
Capital expenditure had dipped to ₹3.43 lakh crore in FY 2020-21

key_amounts.context: capital expenditure in FY 2022-23
capital expenditure as percentage of total expenditure
increase in capital expenditure over previous year

question: What was the GDP of India for FY 2022-23 and what was the growth rate?

expected.answer_summary: The GDP at current prices (nominal GDP) for FY 2022-23 was ₹269.50 lakh crore, representing a growth rate of 14.21% over the previous year. The GDP at constant prices (real GDP) was ₹160.71 lakh crore with a growth rate of 6.99%.
key_facts: GDP at current prices was ₹269.50 lakh crore in FY 2022-23
Growth rate at current prices was 14.21%
GDP at constant prices was ₹160.71 lakh crore
Real GDP growth rate was 6.99%

key_amounts.context: GDP at current prices for FY 2022-23
GDP growth rate at current prices

question: What was the total revenue receipt collection in FY 2022-23 compared to budget estimates?

expected.answer_summary: Total revenue receipt collection in FY 2022-23 was ₹27.13 lakh crore against the budgeted ₹25.17 lakh crore, representing 7.79% higher than estimates. Gross Tax Revenue was ₹30.54 lakh crore (10.73% higher than BE), and Non-tax revenue was ₹6.07 lakh crore (5.38% higher than BE).
key_facts: Total revenue receipts were ₹27.13 lakh crore
Budget estimate was ₹25.17 lakh crore
Actual was 7.79% higher than budget estimates
Gross Tax Revenue was ₹30.54 lakh crore
Non-tax revenue was ₹6.07 lakh crore

key_amounts.context: total revenue receipts in FY 2022-23
Gross Tax Revenue in FY 2022-23
revenue receipts higher than budget estimates

question: What were the key findings on disclosure and transparency issues in the CAG report?

expected.answer_summary: The CAG report identified several disclosure and transparency issues: (1) Inconsistency in arrears of interest receipts between D2 Form and UGFA 2022-23 - difference of ₹3,162.56 crore for State/UT Government and ₹214.12 crore for PSUs; (2) Variation in financial assets (loans) disclosed in D4 Statement vs Receipt Budget - loans to States/UTs showed ₹0.05 lakh crore in D4 vs ₹5.82 lakh crore in Statement 1(ii); (3) Variations in BE figures between H1/H2 statements and Annual Financial Statement; (4) Variations in BE figures between MTFP Statement and AFS.
key_facts: Arrears of interest from State/UT Government showed difference of ₹3,162.56 crore
Arrears from PSUs showed difference of ₹214.12 crore
Loans to States/UTs showed ₹0.05 lakh crore in D4 vs ₹5.82 lakh crore in Receipt Budget
BE figures varied between H1/H2 statements and AFS
Ministry guidelines issued to ensure UBIS data tallies with Finance Accounts

key_amounts.context: difference in arrears of interest from State/UT Government
difference in arrears from PSUs

question: What was the interest payment to revenue receipts ratio and what does it indicate?

expected.answer_summary: The interest payments to revenue receipts ratio was 35.35% in FY 2022-23, increased from 33.99% in FY 2021-22. It had peaked at 38.66% in FY 2020-21. Interest paid on debt was ₹9.59 lakh crore and total revenue receipts were ₹27.13 lakh crore. This ratio measures how much of the Government's revenue is used to pay interest on its debt, indicating fiscal health. The increase shows Government used a comparatively larger part of revenue receipts towards interest obligations.
key_facts: Interest payment to revenue receipt ratio was 35.35% in FY 2022-23
Ratio increased from 33.99% in FY 2021-22
Ratio had peaked at 38.66% in FY 2020-21
Interest paid on debt was ₹9.59 lakh crore
Total revenue receipts were ₹27.13 lakh crore
This is an important indicator of Government's fiscal health

key_amounts.context: interest payment to revenue receipt ratio in FY 2022-23
interest paid on debt in FY 2022-23
interest payment to revenue receipt ratio at peak in FY 2020-21

question: What was the disinvestment receipt in FY 2022-23 and how did it compare to budget estimates and previous year?

expected.answer_summary: Disinvestment receipts in FY 2022-23 were ₹30,184 crore, compared to ₹8,432 crore in FY 2021-22 but lower than the budget estimate of ₹0.65 lakh crore (₹65,000 crore). The receipts were also lower than the pre-pandemic period. Recovery of loans and advances was ₹0.36 lakh crore against BE of ₹0.14 lakh crore.
key_facts: Disinvestment receipts were ₹30,184 crore in FY 2022-23
Previous year receipts were ₹8,432 crore
Budget estimate was ₹0.65 lakh crore
Receipts were lower than pre-pandemic period
Recovery of loans was ₹0.36 lakh crore against BE of ₹0.14 lakh crore

key_amounts.context: disinvestment receipts in FY 2022-23
disinvestment receipts in FY 2021-22
budget estimate for disinvestment

question: What is the debt stabilization indicator for FY 2022-23 and what does it signify?

expected.answer_summary: The debt stabilization indicator for FY 2022-23 was positive at 4.05, indicating that the debt-GDP ratio would eventually fall. This is calculated as Quantum Spread (12.02) plus Primary Deficit (-7.97). A positive indicator is favorable for debt sustainability. The indicator was negative (-21.79) in FY 2020-21 and turned positive (7.98) in FY 2021-22, continuing to be positive in FY 2022-23.
key_facts: Debt stabilization indicator was positive at 4.05 in FY 2022-23
Positive indicator means debt-GDP ratio would eventually fall
Quantum Spread was 12.02
Primary Deficit was -7.97 (₹7.97 lakh crore)
Indicator was -21.79 in FY 2020-21
This indicates favorable conditions for debt sustainability

key_amounts.context: debt stabilization indicator for FY 2022-23
Quantum Spread
Primary Deficit

question: What was the expenditure on focus areas like Agriculture, Education, Health in FY 2022-23 compared to budget estimates?

expected.answer_summary: Expenditure on focus areas in FY 2022-23 compared to BE showed mixed results: Agriculture - ₹1,10,150.69 crore (16.88% lower than BE); Education - ₹1,50,135.02 crore (9.90% lower than BE); Health - ₹1,01,417.81 crore (13.06% lower than BE); Drinking Water and Sanitation - ₹71,991.92 crore (53.09% lower than BE); and Rural Development - ₹3,33,079.93 crore (33.12% higher than BE).
key_facts: Agriculture expenditure was ₹1,10,150.69 crore (16.88% lower than BE)
Education expenditure was ₹1,50,135.02 crore (9.90% lower than BE)
Health expenditure was ₹1,01,417.81 crore (13.06% lower than BE)
Drinking Water and Sanitation was ₹71,991.92 crore (53.09% lower than BE)
Rural Development was ₹3,33,079.93 crore (33.12% higher than BE)

key_amounts.context: Agriculture expenditure in FY 2022-23
Education expenditure in FY 2022-23
Health expenditure in FY 2022-23
Rural Development expenditure in FY 2022-23

question: What is the Extra Budgetary Resources (EBR) and what issues were identified with EBR disclosures?

expected.answer_summary: Extra Budgetary Resources (EBR) are financial liabilities raised by PSUs for which repayment is done from Union Government Budget. These are not reflected in Demand for Grants or Annual Financial Statement. At end of FY 2022-23, EBR was ₹1.38 lakh crore. Issues identified: (1) Unreconciled variation of ₹1,418.70 crore between Statement 27 figures in Expenditure Profile 2023-24 and 2024-25; (2) NSSF loan of ₹350 crore to MTNL shown as outstanding in UGFA 2022-23 was not properly depicted in Statement 27.
key_facts: EBR are borrowings by PSUs serviced from Union Budget
EBR was ₹1.38 lakh crore at end of FY 2022-23
Variation of ₹1,418.70 crore between consecutive Statement 27
Ministry acknowledged inadvertent errors
NSSF loan of ₹350 crore to MTNL was outstanding
EBR not reflected in Demand for Grants or AFS

key_amounts.context: EBR outstanding at end of FY 2022-23
unreconciled variation in EBR figures
NSSF loan to MTNL
